image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |
raw_comment:
1.  The rate of wind and solar have been becoming higher since 2000, but fossil fuels occupies more than half of materials. In fact, the situation is seen as a problem, so the governments of various countries are promoting renewable energy. I had an opportunity to think about a solution to improve the situations when I was a junior high school student. 
2. I wonder why the electric power generation in the world decreased in around 2008 and 2021. I think that the Lehman shock in 2008 and the spreading of coronavirus relate to the result. Indeed, the economy was in a slump because of them, so demand of electric power generation decreased. 
3. If the rate of fossil fuels remains steady, the global warming and the air pollution will worsen. So, the precious natural environment in Akita, where I live, could be negatively affected. For instance, the growth of Akita cedars may deteriorate. 
4. Catchy headline : What is the Solution to Increase the Rate of Clean Energy?

raw_comment:
Overall, electricity generation has increased over time. While the use of clean energy sources globally has grown over the last 20 years, the use of fossil fuels has been consistently higher. Coal and gas are the most popular sources, and wind/solar power has experienced the most growth recently. 

I wonder why, even with the proliferation of new ways to generate electricity, the use of gas and coal has steadily increased. It must have something to do with the accessibility of the resources, but I wonder how much the growth of global population effects how electricity is generated. I wonder how greatly these statistics vary from country to country. 

Canvassing for the local democratic campaign, I noticed that clean energy was a very important issue. Everyone is impacted by the negative effects brought about by climate change, which the expenditure of fossil fuels has been proven to greatly contribute to global warming. 

Good Ole’ Coal: It’s Electric!

raw_comment: Something that I noticed is that there is a graph that has a y-axis that is counted in gigawatt hours and an x-axis that is labeled in years. Additionally, there are tiered color blocks on the graph that each symbolize a different fuel for electricity. Another point is that the yellow-orange colored blocks are made of a cleaner fuel alternative and the black-gray blacks represent fossil fuel-based electricity options. Something that I wonder is how much a gigawatt is and what the best thing to compare it to would be in order to comprehend the size of it better. Another thing I wonder is what some of the differences between clean and fossil-based electricity are. This data affects the community around me because fossil fuels are a large contributor to climate change so it is important to see how much energy we are basing on fossil fuels versus cleaner fuel options. One headline for this graph could be, “Global Use and Differences in the Electricity Generation”

raw_comment:
I've seen that the production of power has been rising over time. In addition to coal being the main source of emissions that may harm the environment, clean generators are becoming more and more common. I could also see that China generates twice as much power as the United States and more than five times as much as India.

I wonder how we can ever rescue life on this planet given that people are addicted to non-sustainable lifestyles, that the fossil fuel business and electric utilities are primarily concerned with gaining power and profit, and that the government is frightened to take the necessary steps to regulate the answer.

This is relevant to my community because everything runs on power, and we need to take care of it by maintaining a healthy, non-toxic atmosphere and stocking it with clean supplies.

A Whole New Era of Eco-Friendly Power

I notice that we have added no meaningful nuclear capacity in the last quarter century despite it being an incredibly easy fix for both massive power generation needs and essentially zero carbon emissions. And there is a class of "environmentalist" that actually fights FOR that outcome. And we can thank them for the concomittant absolute (if not percentage) rise in coal and other fossil fuels to meet growing energy demand.

Solar is great, but nuclear is a magic bullet that's been around for decades and has only improved since. We are being foolish.

raw_comment:
We evaluated a 2022 graph titled “World electricity generation” by Ember. We noticed that it displayed the types and amounts of energy used throughout the world as a whole from 2000 to the 2020s. There were also separate graphs that specifically documented the energy usage of the United States, China, and India over the same time period. We noted that the overall energy usage worldwide for all types is going up, and that nuclear is the most prominent energy source while coal is the least prominent. We also noticed that there were “dips” in worldwide energy usage around 2008 and 2020, which probably correspond with the economic hardships caused by the 2008 housing crisis and the coronavirus pandemic. 

 

We wonder what the data looked like before 2000, as “clean” energy seems to be a relatively new phenomenon. We also wonder why India’s energy usage is so low when compared to the United States and China.

 

This topic is important because we all use energy every day, and it is useful to know the main types of energy being used. It also gives more insight into how different countries operate.

 

Our title for the graph is “Ember’s Evaluation of the Evolution of Electricity.”

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

What I noticed: Our use of electric energy has almost doubled since 2000.- alarming! Utilities are still using fossil fuels way more than clean energy - 3x times more! - to create our electricity.

What I wonder: How will we ever save life on this planet with humanity’s addiction to a lifestyle that is not sustainable, electric utilities and the fossil fuel industry focused on amassing wealth and power, and government afraid to make the needed moves to regulate the solution.

How it will affect my community: Quality of life will fall slowly and then quickly for all my neighbors and me, and existential despair will be rampant.

raw_comment:
All types of electricity generation have been on the rise in terms of total electricity produces since the year 2000. Of the forms of electricity generation, clean energy produces the most, with nuclear and hydroelectric electricity taking the #1 and #2 spots. Among fossil fuels, oil and gas produce the most electricity, but not nearly as much as clean forms of energy such as in nuclear or hydroelectric forms. 

I wonder how the total electricity generated for each form of generation depends on the energy production of each country. China seems to be on the rise and most likely will still continue generating more and more electricity, with a high emphasis on fossil fuels. The USA has remained rather constant, and is interesting because there seems to be a downtrend in fossil fuels. Could this be because they source fossil fuels from other countries?

I live in the USA, and so the graph showing the forms of electrical generation in the US is interesting to me. It shows that gas has been becoming the main source of electrical power for the US, as well as coal. Clean forms of energy generation like solar power have only contributed a speck to the total energy production, despite it being made a huge deal nowadays.

Headline:

raw_comment:
The world's electricity generation has been steadily climbing for the past 25 years, with a particular increase in clean energy. China produces the more energy than the US, with India producing less than both.
I wonder why India produces so much less energy than China despite the vast population living in both countries. Maybe China is more urbanized and industrialized than India. I also find it interesting that the United States has a small amount of people compared to both China and India, yet produces so much energy.
As someone who lives on Earth during the current climate crisis, it is somewhat relieving to see the amount of clean energy being produced. This data gives me some comfort that people are working together to find new ways to mobilize society in a safe and clean way.
Headline: RISE AND SHINE, WORLD

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

raw_comment:
This graph shows the World's Electricity Generation from 2000-2022, specifies where this energy is derived from, and if that energy is clean or fossil. I notice that there are more gigawatt hours of fossil electricity such as gas, oil, and coal than clean electricity. This is expected given the global warming crisis that we are currently facing as a result of these fossil fuels being emitted into the air in order to generate electricity. However, I also noticed that clean energy has significantly increased since 2000, especially wind and solar electricity which faced a significant increase. As climate change has become a more real and pressing issue over the past couple of years, there has been a bigger push to use renewable energy such as putting solar panels on your roof. The more specific graphs demonstrate electric generation in countries like China, India, and the U.S. I noticed that China used significantly more electricity than India even though they have the same population. China also generates most of its electricity through coal. This makes sense considering how industrialized China is and how many factories it needs to upkeep. I can infer this is why China has such bad air quality and pollution. The graph of the U.S. concerns me because while our population is much less than India's, we still use more electricity than them. I wonder what other innovations we will develop to limit fossil electricity generation.
"The Future is Clean!"

raw_comment: The first thing I notice in the first graph "World Electricity Generation", is that over time there has been an overall steady increase in both clean and fossil fuel gigawatt hours. I additionally noticed that even over time, coal and gas, both very harmful to the environment, are still the most used. I wonder if gas and coal are more affordable, in comparison to clean electricity. In regards to the second group of graphs, I noticed all three countries given have very different graphs. China's graph shows a very steep increase in electricity generation. The United States graph displays a consistent generation of both fossil and clean electricity. India's graph shows very little generation of electricity at all. I wonder if the generation of electricity is affected by the country's economy or level of technological advancement. All three of these countries are all very populated, so that could be why they all still rely on fossil fuels. This relates to me and my community, as it is important to acknowledge the need for clean energy to preserve our environment. "Clean Countries or Coal Countries"

raw_comment:
I see a rise in electricity generation and a positive correlation with years in these graphs. I noted that, as the graphs of China, India, and the US demonstrate, this increase is not necessarily uniform across all nations. China's electrical production expanded year after year between 2000 and 2020 at a high linear pace, while the US increased only slightly throughout that time. I can also see that each nation uses coal as its main source of electricity generation, but over time, the US has been using less of it while the other two have both been using more. 

 I wonder how the United States has managed to maintain steady electricity generation in spite of economic and population growth. Although China has a much greater population, the huge difference in electrical generation is still confusing. This raises the question of whether environmental laws and regulations are in force in nations other than the United States.


This relates to my town since switching to renewable energy offers many advantages, especially in terms of lowering air pollution and enhancing general health for a large number of people.

How do China, the U.S., and India generate their electricity?

Thanks to all the students who responded.   Here are some of the catchy headlines that we received:  "The Rise of Renewables: Wind and Solar Power Surge Forward in the Global Energy Mix" by Isabelle of California Polytechnic State University, San Luis Obispo; “Our World's Powerhouse: Clean Energy and Fossil Fuels” by Esmeralda of Perth Amboy High School in N.J.; “The World's Electricity: Clean or Dirty?” by Melina of Marble Hill High School for International Studies in the Bronx, N.Y.; and three headlines from California:  "Power Dynamics: Global Energy Shift Towards Renewables, Challenges Fossil Fuel Reliance" by Emma ; "Fueling the Future: Cleaning Up Our Energy" by Jackson and "Powering Progress: The Rise of Renewable Energy" by Curtis.

Please return on Friday when we will reveal the free link to the article that includes this graph.  Also in the Reveal are a summary of the topic, additional questions, shout outs for some of the best headlines for the graph and Stat Nuggets -- definitions of words that relate to the graph and where you can find them in the graph.

The next graph will be released by Friday, April 12 and live-moderated on Wednesday, April 17.  The topic – shrinkflation!

Signing off – Sharon Hessney from Boston, who wonders if we can generate and transmit enough electricity for all of the electrical items we have now, and Rebecca Nichols from Anchorage, who enjoyed her first time moderating!

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

Why does the World need more energy on the daily?

I notice that fossil fuels are the main source of energy in the world with coal leading. Also wind and solar energy are used the least with nuclear and hydroelectric energy being used more.

I wonder how fossil fuels are still the main source of energy especially since our fossil fuels are expected to run out in the near future.

This relates to me and my community because debates of what energy is safe and should be used by the US to keep the environment more healthy.

raw_comment: In the four graphs, I notice that China’s graph is pretty high compared to India and the U.S. The other thing I recognized was how low the graph was for India, considering that the population of India is pretty high. The graph is so low, so that came as a surprise to see. The other thing I noticed was how leveled out the U.S. was in the graph, which was also surprising because even though our population is not as high as India's and China's, we use a lot of those products. I wonder why India's graph is so low for the number of people who live there. I also wonder why China uses much more than India, even though India has a high population. If I were to pick a catchy headline for the graphs, it would be, “The Big Three: Clean and Fossil Fuel.”

raw_comment: When looking at this graph, we notice that it is about electricity. Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, and wind and solar, are increasing more than coal and gas, which have remained similar gigawatt hours each year since 2000. It is interesting to see that the clean electricity is rising more in recent years as compared to the fossil electricity which is at a slower increase. I wonder if this will continue to happen, and if the fossil electricities such as coal or gas will begin decreasing in the coming years. I also wonder the reason for the difference in inclines when comparing clean and fossil electricity. This relates to me and my community because these me and the people around me are users of electricity in several shapes and forms. We all use it every day and it is important to be educated on it.

I also wonder how much more expensive it is to produce electricity from clean sources compared to fossil fuels?This affects my community because it would be better for everyone  if we continue to use clean energy sources over fossil fuels. In addition, clean energy sources are more sustainable and efficient than the current coal-based electricity system because they generate a greater quantity of electricity over a longer period of time.

In 2020, they used around 2 million gigawatt hours of electricity. I found this surprising, as India is a heavily populated country. But, I wonder if the poverty in India may have impacted the results of this graph. This relates to my community because fossil fuels are harmful to the environment. Through this graph, we can see that countries like China are using a large amount of fossil fuels. It is important that nations around the world try to shift towards cleaner energy. I would title this graph: “Changes in the World Electricity Generation.”

raw_comment:
In the world graph I noticed that fossil fuels remain as the source that generates the most amount of electricity from 2000-2020. The electricity generation has increased slightly for gas and coal. However, the electricity generation remained generally the same for nuclear energy at about 2 million gigawatt hours and hydroelectric sources at producing about 5 million gigawatt hours. Oil and other sources have remained constant as well at around 1 million gigawatt hours. I also noticed that the generation of electricity for wind and solar has greatly increased. From 2000-2o10 wind and solar were barely used to produce electricity, but there has been a steady rise to about 4 million gigawatt hours in 2020. Furthermore, for the country totals graphs, I noticed that the use of coal has increased in China and India, but has decreased in the United States. I wonder how the data will change once countries start to come closer to their climate change goals (will there truly be an increase in generation of electricity from clean energy than with fossil fuels?) I wonder what the data would be prior to 2000? Why is the United States more steady in electricity generation from 2000-2020 in comparison to China and India? This relates to my community because whether the United States generates electricity with clean power or fossil fuels will impact the economy and economic development. 

“Shocking News: Coal Remains as the Top Producer of Energy in the World”

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

We notice that the use of fossil fuels is consistent, while clean energy is increasing. We also notice that the use of overall energy use has increased over time as well.  Moreover, China and the United States have a large portion of their energy coming from clean sources while India basically gets all their energy from fossil fuels.

What do you wonder? What are you curious about that comes from what you notice in the graphs? 
Why is China using more energy compared to India when both of these countries have a very similar population size is it because China is still a developing country?

What impact does this topic have on you and your community?
This topic impacts our community because it emphasizes the importance of clean renewable energy. By looking at the graphs we can say that the globe is moving towards clean energy, although fossil fuels seem to be the main source of energy. This is important because it suggests that we are working to combat global issues such as climate change. Furthermore, it will encourage people to be thoughtful about their carbon footprint.
On the graph,  US reliance on fossil fuels decreases as the years go by.  As US citizens this could indicate a change in how we source energy as well as utilize energy, influencing our way of life.

raw_comment:
I notice that world electricity generation has increased since 2000, and that clean energy has become more prevalent (rising from 5 to 15 million GWh). I also notice that China had a dramatic increase in electricity use, though the use of coal (a fossil fuel) is the predominant source of power. In contrast, the United States has used less coal since 2000 (2 to 0.4 million GWh). India has generated far less electricity than China or the US--- only around 2 million GWh in 2020.

I wonder why world electricity generation has become increasingly clean. Is it due to international legislation, or because the cleaner resources have become cheaper? I also wonder if the electricity generation in China is increasing because they are a developing nation. I am very surprised that India has such low electricity generation being such a large nation, but I am curious if China's graph was similar prior to 2000.

This relates to me and my community because electricity generation will either pollute or conserve the environment. Fossil fuels such as oil, gas, and coal spur environmental degradation. Therefore, it is important that world electricity generation becomes increasingly clean so that increased worldwide development does not make climate change worse.

"CURRENT News: World Electricity Generation Changes"

raw_comment: These graphs are very intriguing to me, as I have recently been learning a lot about wind and solar energy. Overall, the first graph shows the huge amount of energy consumed across the globe. Energy consumption seems to be on a rapidly rising trend, and although it does not show up to 2024, I am sure the trend will continue upward. I was shocked at how low India's consumption was considering the huge number of people they have. India is more populated than the US and uses significantly less energy. China is similar in population to India but uses so much more energy. I was also surprised that nuclear energy is clean energy. I feel that the only things I hear regarding the word 'nuclear' are negative, so it is surprising that it is associated with something I think of as good and helpful like clean energy. I would title these graphs "Energy Consumption: Where does your country fall?"

raw_comment:
There are 2 graphs displayed here. The first graph displays the world electricity in the clean category: nuclear, hydroelectric and other, wind, solar and the fossil category oil, gas, and coal from years 2000 to 2020. I noticed there was a large increase in the use of cleaner energy and fossil fuels in recent years. I wonder how those environmentalists or those who care about the environment feel about the increased use of fossil fuel emissions and how that affects the environment. Does using clean energy help? The second graph below depicts the total electricity generation graphs for China, the U.S. and India with the year range of 2000 to 2020. I notice that China produces the most coal and India produces the least. I wonder why China produces more fossil fuels than the US and India and how that affects the environment and pollution. I wonder if population has an affect on the mass production of fossil fuels in China and India's because the current population in both of those countries about 1.4 billion each. With the same population you would think they would produce the same amount of fuel. This relates to me and my community as my dad owns an electric car which is environmental friendly and I have been more aware of how much fuel and gas costs affect the world around me.

raw_comment: In this graph, it is measuring the amount of world electricity generation from different types of clean and fossil fuels in gigawatt hours over the years 2000-2020. I noticed that for every type of fuel, there was a general increase in the amount of gigawatt hours of electricity generated over time. I wonder if this is due to advancements in technology for each type of energy source. Also, I noticed that for all 20 years, the clean energy sources had the highest electricity generation with nuclear being the highest reaching over 25 million gigawatt hours in 2020. Out of the clean energy sources, I noticed that wind and solar energy had the lowest electricity generation, and I wonder if this is because they are not as reliable sources as something like nuclear energy since they depend on the weather. Additionally, I noticed that coal had the lowest world electricity generation out of both fossil fuels and all types of fuels, approximating 8 million gigawatt hours around the year 2020. I wonder if it had such low gigawatt hours compared to other sources because it has become less and less popular over the years due to its harmful environmental risks. This relates to my community because I have noticed a greater implementation of clean, renewable energy sources as climate change has become a greater issue. My headline for this graph would be: “The Future of Clean Energy Awaits”.

raw_comment: In the graph, the three countries presented are the three most populated in the world. China and the U.S. produce the most energy, while China and India have the greatest demand for it. I also noticed that China and India rely most heavily on energy produced by coal, while the U.S. is primarily oil and nuclear. I wondered why that is. I presume it is because coal is cheap so it is more affordable for China and India to produce energy for such large populations. An additional thought comes to my mind as well. Why does the U.S. go almost fully nuclear, and build more nuclear plants around the country. It is currently the largest energy producer while also being the cleanest. China, for example, has begun construction of 22 power plants, so why don't we construct more? With the overall cost of living increasing everywhere, it would be more beneficial for the U.S. to construct more nuclear power plants to provide cheaper electricity.

raw_comment: This graph charts the source of the world’s electricity supply based on the method of its production--that is, whether the electricity was produced via fossil fuel consumption in the form of oil, gas, and coal; or, if clean sources like hydroelectric, wind, solar, and nuclear power were used in production. The lion’s share of global power production, as the graph indicates, originates in the burning of coal, and, more broadly, other fossil fuels. This is despite an evident recent uptick in reliance on clean energy sources, perhaps spurred on by recent ecocentric movements and such measures as the Paris Accords. Indeed, the pressures to which such a move responds seem pressing, so much so that the global predominance of coal energy sources--which produce the greatest amount of and therefore most harmful greenhouse emissions--incites no little concern. This concerning trend finds further grounding in the subsequent graphs included below, wherein the global energy consumption is broken apart into a few constituent countries’. The graph evinces that China, one of the world’s foremost economic and industrial superpowers, remains largely dependent on coal energy for its massive energy consumption, around twice that of the United States. The unfortunate truth of such statistics is that overreliance on coal does not only affect the country producing it, but has devastating environmental ramifications for the entire world.

raw_comment:
I noticed that the generation of electricity is increasing over the years as the world is changing. Additionally, more clean energy is being used as the world changes rather than using fossil fuels. Looking at the three smaller graphs, I notice that each graph contains vastly different amounts of energy use. For reference, India has the least usage while China uses the most.  I wonder why they change what energy is used throughout the years. I also wonder about ways we can lessen the energy consumption to have a decrease in statistics. Lastly I think about the last 3 graphs and how we can even achieve those rates. Even how those rates came to be would be beneficial to analyze a deeper look into how electricity is generated.
This relates to me and my community because we all generate electricity and this in turn consumes energy. This is not to say that energy should not be used at all, but especially fossil fuels can be harmful. In order to do this, there would need to be some sort of intervention. If my community does not begin to reduce their energy consumption, the world will not change for the better. I do not think that it is a matter of one energy being better than another, it has been made common sense that clean energy is, but overall reducing our usage will better support the earth than replacing energy sources. It is a start, but there still can be improvements made.

This to me is a bit surprising because India's population is greater than both the United States and China. raw_comment: The four graphs shown depict energy and electricity use in China, the United States, India, and the worldwide average. The first thing I found interesting about this graph was that the majority of energy being used comes from fossil fuels, which are extremely harmful to the environment. It's also extremely interesting to me how India has such a large population but uses very little energy compared to China and the U.S, and I wonder why that is. India also has continued to use mainly fossil fuels for energy, while China and the U.S seem to be using more renewable resources as time goes on. Out of the three counties, the U.S seems to be the only one cutting back on unsustainable resources like gas and oil. I wonder why other countries continue to use so much fossil fuels to fuel their energy system although its harm to the environment is widely known.

This can be similar to people with a lot of money in our world today and choose not to spend it on anything useful or to help out other people. raw_comment:
These graphs show the universal and individually national use of clean and fossil fuels. In the universal graph, I notice that there is a definite spike in the use of clean energy, which is probably due to growing environmental awareness and research. In the graph depicting China's energy use, it seems that although clean fuel is increasing, fossil fuels are too, and to a much larger extent, especially with the use of coal. The United States seems to be significantly improving on their use of fossil fuels. I notice that India uses very little energy at all. I wonder why this is, especially since India is an extremely large country with a large population. This relates to me and my community because if we don't start caring for our planet more, we will have a much dirtier and unhealthy world to live in and leave for future generations. I hope that government leaders everywhere understand the impact of using such detrimental fossil fuels and understand the benefit of using cleaner energy sources. 
"Reaching a Cleaner Future: Energy Sources Around the World"

raw_comment: I notice that even though India has roughly four times the population compared to the United States, its electricity generation is less than half. Also, China and India have roughly the same number of citizens, yet China's electricity generation is more than four times India's. I also notice that nuclear power usage has almost doubled over the past 24 years. This makes me wonder why such large countries vary so much in their need for electricity. I also wonder if, or when, India will hop on the boat of using cleaner power. Lastly, I wonder what is seen as a "good" amount of electricity generation. Is China doing too much? Is India doing too little? Where does the U.S. fall into the mix? This doesn't necessarily relate to me and my community other than showing how much electricity my country uses. A headline that could describe this graph could be, "The Modern 'Goldilocks.' How Much is Too Much?"

raw_comment:
This graph shows the world’s electrical energy resources and how much is estimated to have been used from 2000 to 2022. Those resources being shown include nuclear, gas, coal, wind, solar, oil, hydroelectric, and others. The second graph shows how much of those resources are being used in various countries. Overall, the world has seen a steady increase in the use of the noted resources to power electricity.  Mostly so in nuclear power which was used around 15 million gigawatt hours in 2000 and jumped to surpassing 25 million gigawatt hours in 2020. Out of the country graphs, China is seen to have the biggest jump in use. Nuclear alone jumped over 8 million gigawatt hours in 20 years. India has the slowest incline while America has remained rather stagnant in the amount of resources and energy being produced since 2000. I wonder why America has not seen a drastic increase in the use of resources the way countries like China have. I wonder if there will be a noticeable increase in oil use with the Willow Project being pursued. This relates to my community because we are all affected by the different uses of electrical power. Both clean power and fossil fuels have their pros and cons for communities. For example, clean power is better for the environment but can be less efficient and more cost-effective. Not every community has the privilege of being able to use better, cleaner energy sources. 

“What Lights Up The World? Our Energy Sources Since the 2000s”

raw_comment: This graph depicts the proportion of how different energy sources are used. Coal and gas are used most, but are also fossil fuels. The learner enemies of Nuclear, hydroelectric, and wind/solar are used far less. I wonder how the unclean sources are still the majority when climate change and environmental health have been such prevalent topics of conversation for years. While it was expected that fossil fuels would still be used more, It is surprising that they still dominate as the main sources of energy. There has been a large upsurge in the interests of the environment, hence the amount of energy produced by clean materials should be rising as years pass. This relates to every community because the health of the planet is the most important thing in preserving the health of the people. We need to care for our environment because it is our only home. It is my hope that change continues to push the use of fossil fuels down. A possible title is “Clean v Fossil; Who’s in Charge?”

raw_comment:
The main thing that I notice is the widths of each of the stacked sections of the graphs. The thing that immediately stands out is the increase over time for the Wind and Solar section across the past decade. Also while coal is also increasing, it is noticeably slower than the increase in Wind and Solar. Looking at the country graphs, what stood out to me was the U.S.'s apparent stagnation in overall production of energy when compared to India and China. This is also combined with a marked reduction in Coal production which is in stark contrast to China which seems to have not slowed down on their coal energy production. 
I wonder why the US and China have such different overall energy profiles. Why is China's production rising at such a rapid rate while the US's has been stable for the past 2 decades?
This is significant to my community because how our electricity is produced directly affects our lives. Cleaner energy production means cleaner air which is overall better for human health. Also, in my area there are currently questions regarding a nuclear power plant that is reaching its EOL. Whether or not it's shut down might affect things like energy prices locally but also contributes to the overall landscape of energy production in the US.
Title: "Global Electricity Production Continues to Rise"

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |
raw_comment:
The first thing I notice about this graph is how much coal and gas contribute to the electricity generated between 2000 and 2022 overall. What I notice about China's graph in particular is that it's electricity production has been on the rise, along with its use of coal and gas. The United States' electricity production is the opposite; its electricity production slightly increased while its use of Coal has significantly decreased. India's electricity production, while on the rise, still relies overwhelmingly on coal, and produces less than a quarter of the electricity China produces, despite having similar populations. 
I wonder why China's use of hydroelectric makes up the majority of its clean energy, while nuclear makes up the majority of the United States' energy. I wonder if the difference is due to the country's philosophies regarding these two power sources, or if its due to the different geography of each country. 
The increasing trend of emitting less carbon emissions relates to our community since a decrease in carbon emissions could slow down the effects of climate change. Although the world produces more electricity, the United States has seen a reduction in the use of coal, while China has seen an increase in clean energy production.
Two titles I suggest would be "Electricity Production Stabilizes in the U.S.", or "Electricity Production on the Rise Around the Globe."

raw_comment:
The first thing that popped out to me about these graphs was the sheer amount of energy generation produced by China. In just the coal industry, China uses more energy than the US uses in all industries combined. Another thing that interested me was the steady proportion of nuclear energy throughout the years. Nuclear power has gone through many recent developments making it extremely less dangerous for the environment, but those advancements aren't reflected in its usage. With issues such as clean energy, I wonder how strongly connotations surrounding certain types of energy effect their usage on large scales. Wind and solar energy appears to be the "least" clean form of clean energy according to the graphs, but among the three main types of clean energy, it is the only form that has seen a large increase in usage over time. Though it could also be due to the fact that nuclear and hydroelectric plants both require large amounts of space and infrastructure in order to construct and maintain. 
Being informed about this information is very important because of the prevalence of energy discussions. Hearing debates and making conclusions from there is an incomplete way of developing a strategy for energy moving forward. By seeing how much each country relies on each form of energy, you are better equipped to talk about how those infrastructures could be changed to better the world going forward.
Title: How the US has Moved Away from Coal Energy.

raw_comment:
Immediately, I noticed how a stacked area chart is being utilized to visualize the data presented. The issue with using a stacked area chart like these graphs is that it is difficult to determine the actual size of one section in the chart since each section is built on top of one another like layers of sediment. This also makes it difficult to visually see trends in specific sections since their baselines are all different and affected by the layers below. I also noticed how it appears clean energy sources have increased in the past 22 years, specifically how much wind and solar have exploded recently. 
I wonder how the U.S. has stayed fairly consistent with energy production when it feels as if electricity has become more and more integral to life each day in the twenty-first century. Similarly, on the opposite side, why has China’s production boomed so much?
These graphs relate to me and my community because as climate change continues and natural fossil fuels begin to run out, the need for sustainable energy sources and better energy consumption practices is more important than ever. Keeping energy use steady, or even decreasing is something that we as a community should strive for. Additionally, by making sure we continue to switch more and more to clean energy production, we are helping the prosperity and longevity of the planet and our own futures. 
Title: “A Decade of the World’s Energy: Is Clean Power the Future?”

raw_comment:
The biggest thing that stands out to me from the four graphs is the increasing usage of coal power in China. In recent years, there's been a large global push towards clean energy in response to severe climate change, but these global trends don't appear whatsoever in China. In fact, the total coal usage from China is more than the total energy usage in the US. This makes sense from a population stand point, but looking at the graphs with that perspective makes me wonder why India's total energy usage is so low compared to China and the US, especially when the population of India is about the same as the population of China, which is much larger than the US. These graphs also make me wonder about how effective global initiatives can really be if there are still large nations not meeting the regulations designed to help clean our planet.
Understanding how our nation produces the energy that we've grown to rely on helps contextualize issues regarding our energy grid. Ideas of severely reducing one form of energy versus another need to be backed by the knowledge of how we are currently relying on that energy. Simply making decisions or forming ideas without knowledge of the implications will lead to ignorant and damaging decisions.
For a title of the graphs, you could use 
"What Powers the People? Energy Production Across the Years"

raw_comment:
Within the World Electricity Generation graph, I notice that the electricity generation in all the given categories increases from 2000 to 2020. It is evident that more electricity is being generated now compared to 20 years ago. However, I also observe that the growth of fossil fuels has been much slower compared to nuclear, hydroelectric, wind, and solar electricity generation methods.
I genuinely wonder how much further the total electricity generated can rise in the upcoming future until it reaches a possible limit. I am curious whether overpopulation could lead to a scarcity of resources to generate a sufficient amount of electricity for the human population.
This impacts the way my community and I must think about preserving energy. Excessive use of the world's fossil fuels deteriorates the cleanliness of our planet. We must strive to keep the Earth clean to ensure that future generations can live comfortably on it. A catchy headline could be "Will the Earth Be Safe with These Electricity Generation Methods?"

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

raw_comment: Switching fully to clean energy is crucial to mitigating environmental damage. The 'World Electricity Generation' graph shows energy trends over 20+ years, revealing a concerning global reliance on coal and gas, despite advances in renewable energy technology. It's alarming that, globally, these non-renewables dominate, even as the US shifts from coal to natural gas—ironic, given America's role in clean energy innovation. This data prompts questions about the fossil fuel industry's influence in maintaining this imbalance, despite its known environmental costs. I hope that eye-opening statistics like this help influence my local policymakers to make better, more environmentally conscious decisions regarding the continued use of fossil fuels. My personal suggested headline for this graph is, “Capturing the Disproportionality Between Renewable and Fossil Fuel Energy Sources - 2000 to 2022.”

raw_comment:
The first thing that I notice is that there is an increase in world electricity generated between 200 and 2022. I also see that the clean sources of energy have reliably produced more energy than the fossil fuel sources of energy. Not only that, but the clean sources seemed to have had a gain of over 10 million gigawatt hours, compared to fossil that has at most had 7 to 8 million gigawatt hours gain. From the second figure, we can see that Chinas total electricity has dramatically increased, where the amount generated has gone from about 1 million gigawatt hours to about 5.5 million for fossil and 7 to 8 million for clean.
This is significantly higher than the other two countries shown: the United States and India. Over the last 20 years, clean fuel has seemed to stay fairly steady in the US. whereas fossil fuel has seemed to decrease. This makes me wonder how population affects the need of fossil fuels compared to clean power. We know that the population of China is significantly larger than the US, so would this be the reason why China's electricity generation has increased so much? If so, why has the US electricity generation increased so little? This relates to my community because we are the new generation. Clean energy is the future and it is our lob and duty to take care of the planet. A catchy headline may be "Are Fossil Fuels a Thing of the Past? - Clean vs Fossil Energy Production from 2000 to 2022"

raw_comment: I noticed how dips in energy production (2008 and 2020) seem to follow years with an economic recession. Additionally, nuclear energy seems to generate the most electricity but the differences between each type are not overwhelmingly different. I enjoyed the gradient with the energy type color coding. Thinking about the use of this graph in a broader sense, it could be tied to climate change. While there is an upward trend within all the energy sources, sources colored “clean” have a more dramatic slope in their trend line.  This impacts me and my community because it provides hope for the future and suggests we are headed in a good direction to work on taking better care of our planet. A catchy title for this graph would be Ticking Towards a Cleaner Future: World Electricity Generation (2000-2024).

   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

raw_comment: The first thing that I notice is that there is an increase in world electricity generated between 200 and 2022. I also see that the clean sources of energy have reliably produced more energy than the fossil fuel sources of energy. Not only that, but the clean sources seemed to have had a gain of over 10 million gigawatt hours, compared to fossil that has at most had 7 to 8 million gigawatt hours gain. From the second figure, we can see that Chinas total electricity has dramatically increased, where the amount generated has gone from about 1 million gigawatt hours to about 5.5 million for fossil and 7 to 8 million for clean. This is significantly higher than the other two countries shown: the United States and India. Over the last 20 years, clean fuel has seemed to stay fairly steady in the US, whereas fossil fuel has seemed to decrease. This makes me wonder how population affects the need of fossil fuels compared to clean power. We know that the population of China is significantly larger than the US, so would this be the reason why China's electricity generation has increased so much? If so, why has the US electricity generation increased so little? This relates to my community because we are the new generation. Clean energy is the future and it is our job and duty to take care of the planet. A catchy headline may be "Are Fossil Fuels a Thing of the Past? – Clean vs Fossil Energy Production from 2000 to 2022"

1. I noticed the graph's overall increasing tendency, as well as the significant increase in energy consumption since 2000. Yet, clean energy has accounted for the vast majority of this energy increase, while coal, gas, and oil have remained relatively stable over the last two decades.
2. I'm curious why China has had such a significant rise in electricity compared to the United States and India. I also wonder what will motivate the entire world to utilize more clean energy than fossil fuels to create electricity.
3. This concerns me and my community because as the world transitions to more clean and renewable energy sources, the pollution from fossil fuels is expected to reduce, which is good for the earth and will benefit all of us. 
4. Changes in Electricity Generation: Is it Changing for the Better?

raw_comment:
When analyzing the graphs depicting total electricity generation in China, the United States, and India, China immediately captured my attention. This is because it displays a tremendous increase in electricity generated in a short amount of time (only about 20 years). This is vastly different from the graph of the United States, which reveals a relatively stable trend in electricity generated. India on the other hand, has the lowest amount of electricity generated, and not only that, but most of their electricity is generated from fossil fuels, instead of clean power sources. The initial graph displaying the global levels of electricity generation indicates that there has been a steady increase over time, accompanied by a noticeable shift towards increased utilization of clean energy sources.
Something I wonder is if China and India both have similar populations, why is China's electricity usage about 4 times larger than India's? I also wonder how the United States electricity usage has remained so consistent throughout the years, even though we have had many new inventions/resources that require an immense amount of power.
The fact that still over half of our electricity production is coming from fossil fuels, is a bit concerning due to the fact of their many harmful impacts on Earth. I know switching to cleaner forms of electricity can be more expensive, but they are overall better for our planet.
Title: "Powering the World: Electricity Generation Over the Last 20 Years"

raw_comment:
What I notice:
Coal has consistently been the largest source of global electricity generation, though it has declined slightly since 2010. Gas, wind and solar, and other renewables have been steadily increasing since 2000. Nuclear and hydro have stayed relatively flat over the 20 year period. Fossil fuels (coal, gas, oil) still make up the vast majority of electricity generation compared to clean sources.

What I wonder:
How much have total global electricity generation amounts changed over this period? The graph shows share but not absolute amounts. What explains the slight dip in coal around 2020 - was it due to the pandemic reducing energy demand? How much variability is there between countries and regions in the electricity source mix? How are these trends projected to change in the coming decades as renewables scale up further?

Impact on me and my community:
The still-dominant share of fossil fuels has major climate change and air pollution impacts that affect the whole world. More renewables means cleaner air and reduced emissions, but also potential economic disruption in fossil-dependent communities. The trends point to the need to massively scale up clean energy investment and infrastructure in my community and globally.

Headline: "Coal still king as renewables rise in powering the world"

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

raw_comment:
The first thing that I noticed was that the United States' energy usage was about half of China's despite the United States having a population less than a fourth of China's population. This means that per person, we use more than twice the amount of energy than China does. This only true for the most recent years too, as in earlier parts of the graph our energy usage per person is even greater compared to China's.
Another thing I noticed was how the type of energy the United States uses changed. We can see a gradual increase in both oil and wind and solar starting around 2010, with a decrease in coal around that same time. While it is good that  the United States is using more wind/solar energy and using less coal energy, I wonder if that will be offset by the increased usage in oil. These shifts in the graph are something that I can connect back to own community. My hometown is a suburban area in California, where over the past 10 years I have observed many households add solar panels to their houses, including my own. So that increase in the graph I can definitely reflect. However, on the other hand, the suburban landscape also dictates the necessitiy for a car, which increases the use of oil. So that is something in the graph that I can also unfortunately reflect in my hometown. What this graph should tell us is that we should limit our oil use, whether it's walking to places or using public transit. A title would be "3 largest countries in the world, how they use energy"

raw_comment: First examining the “World electricity generation” graph demonstrates an evident increase in electricity generation with fossil fuels dominating a majority of the sources of that energy. Of the clean energy, it’s interesting to note that hydroelectric and other is the greatest source of clean energy compared to nuclear and wind/solar. Of the fossil fuels, coal is the greatest source of fossil fuels compared to gas and oil (and other). Another intriguing thing to note is that clean energy seems to be increasing faster than fossil fuels, however, with clean energy sources taking a bigger share of the total world electricity generation as time passes. I wonder how this increase in clean energy will affect the countries’ totals since China seems to follow that trend of clean energy’s greater share of the world total, but the United States’s and India’s totals seem to be changing at slower rates than China’s. With the rise of clean energy, I hope that statistics like these cause us to reflect on how our communities and we use electricity and where our electricity is coming from. I strongly hope there are more talks especially regarding sustainability when it comes to unrenewable sources of energy such as fossil fuels. A catchy headline could be “Our Energy Sources are Electrifying the World - 2000 to 2022”

1) I notice that the world's electricity generation has significantly increased throughout the years. Fossil fuels surpassed 15 million gigawatt hours and renewable sources of power present a positive increase that surpassed 25 million gigawatt hours. Countries like China, the U.S., and India differ in the amount of electricity generation used, the U.S. demonstrates leisurely decreasing its use of fossil fuels while slightly increasing the use of renewable power sources. As China uses the most clean energy out of the U.S. and India they also use a large mass of fossil fuels. On the other hand, India demonstrates the use of the least clean power and fossil fuels under 2 million gigawatt hours. 
2) I wonder how the economy plays a role in the use of electricity generation, China and India a both largely populated, yet India's low electricity generation greatly contrasts the use of electricity generation in China and the U.S. From analyzing how the use of electric generation greatly increased in 20 years how could the data differ in the future? Will we maintain the use of clean power on top?  
3)This relates to my community and me because we as a community contribute to the use of the world's electricity generation, making the resources we use contribute to either the negative impacts that fossil fuels or the positive impacts of using clean energy/power. 
4) Our World's Powerhouse: Clean Energy and Fossil fuels!

3. **Data variables**

| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |
raw_comment:
The first thing I noticed was that the United States' energy usage was only about twice as less as China's usage despite having a population less than a quarter the size of China's. This means that the average energy usage per person in the US is significantly greater than China's and especially India's. I then noticed the change in the type of energy the United States' has used over the years. From 2000 to 2020 there seems to be a decrease in coal, an small but noticeable increase in wind and solar, and then an increase in oil. So while the increased use of the clean wind and solar energy, and the decrease of coal energy looks good, I wonder if it is offset by the dramatic increase in oil. 
As the graph depicts energy usage from 2000 to 2020, I am able to observe if me and my family's energy usage coorelates with the graph. Over the last 20 years many families in my neighborhood, including my own, added solar panels to their roofs, this lines up fairly accurately with when solar and wind power starts to increase (around 2010). I am glad to see this change, however I unfortunately also observe that with the layout of my suburban town, driving is almost essential to go to places so seeing an increase in oil also makes sense. These graphs should encourage people to cut out oil, if they need to go somewhere close and walking/biking is a feasible means of travel then they should consider that instead. My title: "3 largest countries in the world, and how they consume energy"

Compared to the US and India, I've seen that China's rise in power generation has been far greater. Globally, there has been a consistent rise in the production of energy, with wind and solar power growing in importance since 2010. 

I find it hard to understand why there has been a rise in the use of fossil fuels when there are so many businesses fighting to reduce the use of these fuels and campaigners pushing for the switch to renewable energy.

This has an effect since it raises awareness among my community and myself about the source of our electricity and may encourage lobbying for a greater emphasis on the generation of clean energy.

How Is the Global Electricity Supply Changing?

raw_comment:
The graph shows the world electricity generation from the year 2000 to just beyond 2020, with sources split into "clean" and "fossil". Coal, gas, oil and other fossil fuels are represented alongside nuclear, hydroelectric and other clean sources such as wind and solar. There's a visible trend showing a decline in coal use, a relatively steady production from gas, and an increase in wind and solar energy generation over time.

I'm curious about what is behind the decline in coal usage and the increase in wind and solar energy. It could be technological advancements, economic factors, or policy changes. 


This trend reflects a global shift towards more sustainable energy sources which could be influencing local energy policies and infrastructure. Our community is experiencing changes in energy costs(PGE keeps going up!),  so this may reflect,. or be shifted by an increase in renewable energy. It was interesting to see that the US is relatively stagnant for clean energy compared to china.

I would call this graph, "The Rise of Renewables: Wind and Solar Power Surge Forward in the Global Energy Mix"

raw_comment:
In this graph, I notice that there is an overall increase in the trend of world electricity generation, when looking at all sources of electricity. In differentiating between clean sources and fossil fuels, clean sources seem to be much more prevalent in use than fossil fuels, which is a positive consequence. Diving even further into detail, I noticed that the United States has a much more gradual increase in electricity generation than that of China, which has a much steeper slope and more rapid increase in electricity generation.
I wonder why exactly China's electricity generation has increased so much faster than that of the United States. Both China and the United States are massive centers of economic and industrial growth, so it is interesting that China's change in electricity generation is far more drastic than the United States.
Energy sources have a huge impact on me and my surrounding community. It is highly important that we steer away from using fossil fuels as an energy source, as they are detrimental to the environment and the world that we live in, and create more sources of clean energy. The United States' electricity generation increasing at a much slower rate is both good and bad for my community- good in the sense that less fossil fuels are being used, bad in the sense that less clean energy is used. I would title this graph "Fueling the Future- World Electricity Generation from 2000 to 2020".

image_description:
1. **Chart type**  
   - **Stacked area chart** for world electricity generation over time.
   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |
raw_comment:
What do you notice?
-Over the years, China had the steepest increase in electricity generation, for all types of power, compared to the United States and India. 
- In 2000, China had lower amounts of electricity generation than the US but surpassed the US by around 2010. 
- The overall electricity generation in the US has not increased by much over the years, but there is a larger proportion of clean fuels being used instead of fossil fuels (especially coal).
- India’s electricity generation remained lower than both China and the US but has steadily increased over the years. 

What do you wonder?
- I wonder if the increase in electricity generation in China is correlated with how “open” their economy was. I know that China has worked on expanding its private sector. 
- I wonder why US electricity generation has plateaued, and if this phenomenon is also seen in other Western developed countries. 
- What is the relationship between energy generation and the political stability of a country?

How does this relate to you and your community?
- This relates to how I might think/vote about certain energy policies in the US. 
- I see a lot more Teslas in the Bay Area - electric cars don’t seem like they are reserved for the rich anymore; many middle-class folks are buying Teslas to account for rising gas prices.

Create a catchy headline that captures the graph’s main idea.
Our Energy Usage Has Transformed Over the Last 20 Years

raw_comment:
I noticed that worldwide clean electricity sources such as nuclear, wind, solar, and hydroelectric have experienced a greater increase in output of electricity over time compared to fossil fuels. When examining the individual countries' graphs we can see that, for the United States specifically, clean electricity sources have only experienced a relatively slight increase over time. Meanwhile, the United States’ coal usage is the only source that experienced a decrease in generation. 
I am curious as to why the US is less reliant on coal, if it has something to do with the cost of mining coal, or if the US is choosing to prioritize investing in clean sources instead. If the US is in fact choosing to spend more on clean sources of electricity this would mean, policymakers are becoming more conscious of the need to take steps to protect the environment so our planet can be habitable for the next generation. I would title this graph: “Electricity Generation for our Next Generation”.

1) I noticed that China has a greater total electrical generation than the U.S. and India. I also noticed that clean, renewable power tends to be used over fossil fuels.

2) I wonder why China and the U.S. have more electrical generation than India when India has the same population as China. How are they supplying electricity to their over 1 billion population?

| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |

raw_comment:
What do you notice?

Looking at the global scale, the production of electricity has been continuously increasing. This increase is also followed by the rise in the consumption of resources. Despite clean electricity sources having the ability to produce more electricity than fossil fuels, it seems that fossil fuels are more popular in terms of consumption. Coal, for instance, is the most consumed, but generates the least amount of electricity

Wind and solar energy began to receive more traction around 2010 and produces a significant amount of electricity.

Unlike China and India, the fossil fuel consumption in the United States began to decrease around 2010.

What do you wonder?

Why do China and India continue to consume fossil fuels?

Despite having a smaller population, why does India produce less electricity and consume fewer resources than the United States?

What does the steady trend in clean electricity production and the decrease in fossil fuel electricity production say about electricity production in the United States?

How does this relate to you and your community?

This is relevant to me and my community because we can see how much renewable energy sources can generate more electricity. Knowing how much fossil fuels cause pollution, I wonder how much I contribute to the consumption of these resources and how my community and I can avoid it.

Catchy Headline:

"Clean Energy: More Power, Less Consumption - But why?"

raw_comment:
What do you notice?
-Over the years, China had the steepest increase in electricity generation, for all types of power, compared to the United States and India. 
- In 2000, China had lower amounts of electricity generation than the US but surpassed the US by around 2010. 
- The overall electricity generation in the US has not increased by much over the years, but there is a larger proportion of clean fuels being used instead of fossil fuels (especially coal)
- India’s electricity generation remained lower than both China and the US but has steadily increased over the years. 

What do you wonder?
- I wonder if the increase in electricity generation in China is correlated with how “open” their economy was. I know that China has worked on expanding its private sector. 
- I wonder why US electricity generation has plateaued, and if this phenomenon is also seen in other Western developed countries. 
- What is the relationship between energy generation and the political stability of a country?

How does this relate to you and your community?
- This relates to how I might think/vote about certain energy policies in the US. 
- I see a lot more Teslas in the Bay Area - electric cars don’t seem like they are reserved for the rich anymore; many middle-class folks are buying Teslas to account for rising gas prices

raw_comment: After examining the World’s Electricity Generation Graph, I noticed that clean sources produce the most amount of power in the world compared to fossil fuels. More specifically, nuclear sources produce the most overall. Additionally, as the years increase, more and more electricity is being produced from all sources. In the smaller graphs below, I notice that China uses by far the most electricity compared to the United States and India. India produces the least amount of electricity and also uses the least amount of clean energy sources to produce electricity. I wonder if there is a reason as to why India produces the least amount of electricity. India is a very large country with over a billion people, so based on that, I would assume that India would be one of the countries that produces the most amount of electricity. The impact that this has on me and my community is making me wonder how much electricity I use daily and what kind of energy sources produce my electricity. Although, when observing the United States graph I notice that as the years increase, fossil fuel usage decreases. I am pointing this out because based on this observation, the electricity I use is more likely to be powered by clean energy sources. raw_comment:
1) What do you notice? 
I noticed that clean electricity generation has a more significant increase than fossil generation. This is evident due to the nuclear, hydroelectric, and other, wind and solar increasing each by about a little over 10 million. 
2) What do you wonder?
I wonder how this graph was created and what sampling method was used to receive the answers to support this data. 
3) How does this relate to you and your community?
The whole world requires all the generations evident in the graph and my community can receive a better understanding of the increase of these generations. This could help bring awareness of the fossil generations that may need to be lessened and communities can gather to help. 
4) What’s going on in this graph?
Electricity Generations Increasing by Millions Each Year

I also wonder what the color scale means, aside from differentiating the different groups - Is coal the worst fossil fuel as it is put on the bottom and the darkest, and is Nuclear the cleanest source as it is on the top of the clean category and the lightest in color.

This is impactful as it makes my community and I aware of where we are getting our power from, and can possibly incentive the advocacy for more focus on clean energy production

raw_comment:
In these graphs I notice the increase of electrical generation and the positive correlation it has with years. I noticed that this increase isn’t necessarily equal amongst all countries as seen in the graphs of India, China, and the United States. China’s electrical production has grown at a high linear rate every year from 2000 to 2020, while the United States has only slightly grown over the 20 year time span. I also notice that each country’s main source of electrical generation is coal, but the United States has been decreasing coal use over time while the other two countries both have been increasing theirs. 

I wonder how the United States has been able to keep their electrical generation steady while growing in population and economically, but China’s has grown by almost four times. Of course China’s population is much greater but the large discrepancy in electrical generation is puzzling. This leads me to wonder if other countries outside the United States have environmental laws and acts put in place.

I live in California and this relates to my community because we are constantly passing new laws to preserve the environment and eliminate the use of fossil fuels. I have lots of family in India, and that community is being affected by the fossil fuels being burned, changing the climate. Farmers in India have been forced to move from their hometowns because of climate change and resources have been scarce.

“Electrical Generation: Why Nuclear is Good, For Once.”
