raw_comment:
I notice that the United States' electricity generation has barely raised at all since 2000 (with gas and wind/solar power taking much of the share from coal), while the world's total generation has raised by well over 10 million gigawatt hours (GWh). China's generation alone accounts for about 8 million GWh, with India accounting for about 1 million GWh. Furthermore, we see that worldwide nuclear generation has barely moved at all, while hydroelectric looks like it's about doubled. Wind and solar has gone from no generation to about 3 million GWh, gas looks like it's about doubled from 3 to 6 million GWh, and coal has risen from 5 to about 8 million GWh.

I wonder why China's generation has increased so much while the United States remained so similar. China has certainly been developing, but more than quintupling their electricity generation seems absurd. What were they doing before they were generating it themselves? Were they importing all of it? I also wonder why no new nuclear energy is being produced when we know how safe and reliable it is.

As the world looks to move towards renewable energy, we see that fossil fuel consumption is still only increasing worldwide. With climate change impacting the world more and more, we are seeing more extreme weather like the polar vortexes that sweep across the US in winter, plunging the Midwest into wind chills below -40 degrees Fahrenheit.

"Electricity Generation in China Soars to More Than Double the United States"

   - **Faceted stacked area charts (small multiples)** for country totals (China, United States, India).

2. **Layout techniques**  
   - **Stacking**: Electricity generation sources are stacked to show total generation and composition.
   - **Faceting / small multiples**: Separate country panels for China, United States, and India.
   - **Color grouping**: Clean sources use yellow/orange hues; fossil sources use blue/gray hues.

3. **Data variables**

| Data Variable | Type of Variable | Visual Encoding |
|---|---|---|
| Year | Temporal | Position on x-axis |
| Electricity generation | Quantitative | Position on y-axis; stacked area height |
| Energy source / generation type (Nuclear, Hydroelectric and other, Wind and solar, Oil and other, Gas, Coal) | Categorical/nominal | Color/hue; stacked area segments; text labels |
| Power category (Clean, Fossil) | Categorical/nominal | Color grouping in legend/header |
| Country (China, United States, India) | Categorical/nominal | Faceting into separate panels; panel titles |
raw_comment:
I noticed that the world’s electricity generation has significantly increased in the last 20 years, almost doubling from about 15  to 30 million gigawatt hours. The graph also depicts how the world still relies heavily on fossil fuels for more than half of its electricity generation, with coal and gas usage slightly rising. On the other hand, clean energy usage has increased with wind and solar power accounting for a higher proportion of electricity generation.

The other graphs depict how diverse the composition of electricity generation is in various countries. For example,   the US population is relatively small compared to China and India, but the amount of energy generated by India is much lower than the US. 

The graphs create many questions including the reasoning behind the fluctuations in world electricity generation. There are dips in certain years like 2019 and 2008. I wonder if the decline in 2019 could be due to the COVID-19 pandemic and if the dip in 2008 might relate to the economic recession.

Since living in California I have seen a shift in energy consumption just in my hometown. In the last few years, there have been decisions to decommission oil rigs off the coast and developments of new battery storage systems that are working towards being emission-free. Overall, the graphs help depict the story of how electricity generation has changed over the years, therefore a catchy name could be “The Megawatt Movement”.

raw_comment:
I notice that according to the graph clean electricity generation is the most used for electrical generation. These electric generators have been leading since the graphs origin point which started in the year 2000. In comparison natural fossil fuels like coal and gas are on the low end of generation being the two lowest used forms of electricity generation in the world. I wonder about the history behind this graph? Can the growth of the types of electrical generation on this graph be put in a linear model or was there some point in time that coal and gas were leading electrical generation? This relates to my community because as I grow older technology is becoming more and more engraved in human life, which means more and more electricity is being taken in. High use of clean electrical generation can in turn help save the environment rather than its fossil fuel counterpart which has some negative side effects.

Is The Worlds Electricity is Clean or Dirty

raw_comment:
These graphs show total energy generation by country but the text says they exclude imports and exports so they probably don't represent usage by that country. If any of these countries are net importers of energy then their total consumption would be higher, whereas if they're net exporters it then it would be lower. 

We can figure out something about the trends in production though. Overall, energy production has nearly doubled in 22 years. Coal and gas still by far make up the largest part of the market however wind and solar production is increasing at the fastest rate. Nuclear and other seam to have slightly reduced in production and hydro slightly increased.

There are two dips in production at around 2008 and 2019 (less pronounced in coal). I wonder what caused them. Something to do with the GFC and COVID19?

In terms of country level, China is by far producing the most coal and its coal production has increased around 4 times since 2000 and it looks to be the biggest coal producer in the world.

In the US by comparison, coal production has decreased by more than half and oil has increased. Oil and gas combined has stayed fairly steady and still make up more than half of overall production. Overall US energy production has stayed fairly steady over the 22 years.  

India does not produce very much energy at all compared to the other countries and it is mostly coal.

raw_comment: As expected with new technology all over, world electricity generation has steadily increased over the last 20+ years. With this increase, fossil fuels have of course increased as well, but at an encouragingly slower rate. Worldwide, renewable energy sources have have begun to take up a large proportion of the electricity generation. The country-specific plots show some interesting trends. I wonder why the electricity generation in the United States has been mostly flat for two decades, even after all of the 21st century economic development. In my community, I hear about renewable energy all the time through campaigns, and it is encouraging to see in these graphs that the rate of increasing fossil fuels has slowed down dramatically, especially in the U.S. This personally affects me and everyone in my community because renewable energy reduces air pollution, leading better overall health. Headline: "Fueling the Future: Cleaning Up Our Energy"

raw_comment: I notice that a positive correlation exists among all energy sources, with clean energy consumption experiencing a substantial increase in recent years while fossil fuel consumption lags. What specific factors are driving the rapid growth of clean energy consumption globally? How might policy interventions and technological advancements further accelerate the transition to renewable energy, particularly in countries like India? What are the long-term implications of these trends for global energy security, environmental sustainability, and climate change mitigation efforts? These global energy trends impact me and my community through their influence on energy prices, environmental quality, and efforts to mitigate climate change. Embracing renewable energy sources locally could lead to job creation, reduced pollution, and increased resilience to energy supply disruptions, contributing to a more sustainable future. Understanding global energy dynamics helps inform local energy policies and encourages individual actions to support cleaner energy initiatives. raw_comment: I notice that, depending on the countries, there is a different type of relationship between the year and the amount of electricity that is being generated by each of them. For example, China exhibits a strong and fast linear growth between the years 2000 and 2020, whereas India’s is more gradual and the United State’s seems to be staying relatively the same. However, overall all countries seem to be using more clean sources, such as nuclear, than they do fossil. This makes me wonder whether the world is truly getting ‘cleaner’ or if these countries are slowly straying away from using fossil fuels to generate electricity. This is very relevant to me and my community in that nuclear enegery, hydroelectric energy, and other clean energies do not produce air pollution as some fossil fuels are notorious for. In order for my community and I to live safer and healthier lives, these are things to consider. Headline: ‘The Race Between Clean Energy and Fossil Fuels Continues’.

raw_comment: I notice that, depending on the countries, there is a different type of relationship between the year and the amount of electricity that is being generated by each of them. For example, China exhibits a strong and fast linear growth between the years 2000 and 2020, whereas India’s is more gradual and the United State’s seems to be staying relatively the same. However, overall all countries seem to be using more clean sources, such as nuclear, than they do fossil. This makes me wonder whether the world is truly getting ‘cleaner’ or if these countries are slowly straying away from using fossil fuels to generate electricity. This is very relevant to me and my community in that nuclear enegery, hydroelectric energy, and other clean energies do not produce air pollution as some fossil fuels are notorious for. In order for my community and I to live safer and healthier lives, these are things to consider. Headline: ‘The Race Between Clean Energy and Fossil Fuels Continues’.

raw_comment: I noticed that out of China, the United States, and India, China appears to use the most clean energy but all of them mainly use fossil fuels. I wonder what will be the breaking point to cause more places to switch to clean energy. I know that places are trying to switch to just clean energy, but the cost of it can be expensive. This impacts my community because if the U.S. starts to run out of fossil fuels or is unable to purchase fossil fuels from other countries, people may start to struggle to afford using clean energy all the time. A  catchy headline would be "The future towards clean energy."
